metadata.title: Name the Human: User + Problem + 3 Stories
metadata.playlist: Say What It Is — The Conductor Brief
metadata.source: INFO 7375 Assignment 5 brief — The Conductor Brief: Part 2 (16 pts)

beats.narration_text: Hola — Liam, Episode 2 of 7. Part 2 is worth 16 points — the most of any section. It breaks into two parts: 2A is the user profile and 2B is the three user stories. The reason it's worth more is that every strategic decision downstream depends on a real, specific person. If you write 'marketers' in Part 2A, your Part 3 data contract is wrong, your Part 4 phase gates are wrong, and your Part 6 differentiator is wrong. 'Marketers' is not a user. Neither is 'students,' 'businesses,' or 'professionals.' A user is a job title, a situation, and a workaround. This episode covers how to write 2A — the user profile — and 2B — three user stories in the exact format the graders check. Sixteen points. Two deliverables. Let's name the human.

props.segment: Name the Human: User + Problem + 3 Stories
props.command: Write a user profile that names the specific person, their situation, their workaround, and why that workaround is not good enough — then three user stories in the exact format.
props.runningText: 2A + 2B together are 16 pts — every downstream section depends on getting this specific…
output: 2A: job title · situation · workaround · real cost of the workaround
2B: 3 user stories — 'As a [specific role], I want [capability] so that [human outcome]'
Traps: generic user · non-standard format · technical 'outcomes'

beats.narration_text: Part 2A has four fields. Job title or role — specific enough that you could find this person on LinkedIn. Not 'marketer.' 'Email marketing coordinator at a B2C e-commerce company with 10 to 50 employees.' Their situation — what they are doing when they reach for this tool. Not 'they need to analyze data.' 'They are preparing the weekly campaign performance report, mid-task, and they have 14 browser tabs open pulling from four different dashboards.' What they do instead right now — the workaround. This is the most important field in 2A. Your A2 PRD already named this, so pull it directly: 'They copy-paste metrics from each dashboard into a Google Sheet, write a three-paragraph summary, and send it as a Slack message. It takes 45 to 90 minutes every Monday.' And why that workaround is not good enough — name the cost. Time, errors, missed signals, or a decision that gets made too late. 'By the time the summary is ready, the team has already made Tuesday's decisions without Thursday's signal.'

artifactLines: Job title/role: specific enough to find on LinkedIn. Not 'marketer' — 'email marketing coordinator at a B2C e-commerce company with 10–50 employees.'
Situation: what they're doing when they reach for this. 'Preparing the weekly campaign report, mid-task, 14 tabs open across 4 dashboards.'
Workaround: what they do instead right now. Pull this from your A2 PRD. Name the tool, the steps, and the time it takes.
Cost of workaround: time / errors / missed signals / decisions made too late. Honest and specific — not a strawman.
Honest current-state is graded. Describe the real workaround and its real cost.
props.sparkLine: Name the workaround. Name its cost.

beats.narration_text: The brief says 'honest current-state' for a reason. Graders have read hundreds of product docs. They can tell when the workaround is a strawman — a made-up worst-case designed to make the solution look better. 'Currently, people manually search Google for hours every day' is a strawman. The real workaround is usually not that bad — it is a reasonable process that works 80 percent of the time and breaks on the remaining 20. Describe that. 'They use a shared Airtable base that works for tracking, but requires manual tagging by three different team members, and when any one of them is out, the tags get inconsistent and the reports run slow.' That is a real workaround. It is functional. It has a real failure mode. Writing the real workaround does two things: it makes your user story feel credible, and it forces you to articulate the specific case where your tool does something the workaround cannot. That specificity is what Part 6 — the differentiator — is built on.

props.artifactHeading: The real workaround — not a strawman
artifactLines: Strawman: 'Currently, people manually search Google for hours every day.' No grader believes this.
Real workaround: functional 80% of the time, breaks on the remaining 20%. Describe that break.
Example: 'Shared Airtable base works for tracking — but requires manual tagging by 3 people. When one is out, tags get inconsistent and reports run slow.'
Why this matters: the specific failure mode of the workaround is exactly where your tool wins.
Honest current-state is graded. A strawman costs points. The real story is always more interesting.
props.sparkLine: The real workaround is functional 80% of the time and breaks on the 20%.

beats.narration_text: Part 2B is three user stories in the exact format. The format is: 'As a [specific role], I want [real capability] so that [human outcome].' Three words that must be right: 'specific role,' 'real capability,' and 'human outcome.' Specific role means you cannot write 'user' or 'team member' or 'administrator.' Write the actual role from 2A. Real capability is what the tool enables — one action, not a feature list. Human outcome is what the person can do or decide after — not a system behavior. The three stories cover three different use cases. Story one is the core job — the primary reason the user reaches for this tool. Story two is a secondary use case — a different context or a different role using the same tool. Story three is a power-user edge case — what the most demanding user does with the tool, the boundary condition. The grader checks: are the roles real? Are the outcomes human? If a story says 'so that the system automatically processes the data,' that is a technical outcome, not a human one.

props.artifactHeading: As a [specific role], I want [capability] so that [human outcome]
artifactLines: Format is exact: 'As a [specific role], I want [real capability] so that [human outcome].'
Story 1 — Core job: the primary reason this user reaches for the tool.
Story 2 — Secondary use case: a different context or a different role, same tool.
Story 3 — Power-user edge case: the most demanding use, the boundary condition.
'Specific role' means no 'user' or 'admin' — write the actual role from 2A.
'Human outcome' = what they can DO or DECIDE after — not 'so that the system processes the data.'
props.sparkLine: Roles must be real. Outcomes must be human.

beats.narration_text: The traps for Part 2 cost real points. Generic user in 2A: minus five for the section and a rewrite before A6. Story in non-standard format: minus five per story — if the grader can't tell which slot is which, the story doesn't count. Technical outcomes in 2B: 'so that the API returns a structured response' is a system event, not a human outcome. Rewrite until you name what a person does or decides. The coherence check for Part 2 is simple: do the stories match the user profile? If 2A names an email marketing coordinator and Story 2 says 'as a data scientist,' you have inconsistent users. The stories should all make sense for the person you named in 2A, or you need to decide which user you are building for and update 2A. The way to catch this before submission is to read your Part 1 sentence, then read your Part 2A user, and ask: is this the exact person that sentence is for? If the user in 2A would not say the sentence in Part 1, you have a mismatch.

artifactLines: Generic user in 2A: −5 pts + rewrite request before A6.
Non-standard story format: −5 per story. If the grader can't tell which slot is which, it doesn't count.
Technical outcomes: 'so that the API returns a structured response' is not a human outcome.
Incoherent users: 2A names one role; stories name a different one → pick one and update both.
Coherence check: read your Part 1 sentence, then your 2A user. Would this person say that sentence? If not, there's a mismatch.
props.sparkLine: Part 1 sentence + Part 2A user must describe the same person.

beats.narration_text: Verdict for Part 2. Part 2A has four fields: specific job title or role, their situation mid-task, the real workaround with the tools and steps and time cost, and the honest reason the workaround is not good enough. Honest current-state is graded — a strawman costs points. Part 2B has three user stories in the exact format: 'As a [specific role], I want [real capability] so that [human outcome].' Three stories: core job, secondary use case, power-user edge case. Traps: generic user, non-standard format, technical outcomes. Coherence check: the user in 2A must be the same person your Part 1 sentence is for. Your turn prompt is in the next beat — it converts your A2 PRD into the user profile and three stories in one run.

props.artifactTitle: Name the Human: User + Problem + 3 Stories
artifactLines: 2A: specific job title · situation mid-task · real workaround (tools + steps + time) · honest cost.
Honest current-state is graded — describe the real workaround, not a strawman.
2B format (exact): 'As a [specific role], I want [real capability] so that [human outcome].'
3 stories: core job · secondary use case · power-user edge case.
Traps: generic user (−5) · non-standard format (−5/story) · technical outcomes.
Coherence check: 2A user = the person your Part 1 sentence describes. Mismatch = update both.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Paste this prompt into Claude. Fill in the brackets with your actual A2 content before running. The prompt reads: Here is my A2 problem statement: [paste it]. Here is the user I described in A2: [paste the user description from your A2]. Generate Part 2A of the Conductor Brief with these four fields: the user's specific job title or role, their situation when they reach for this tool, their current workaround with the specific tools and steps and time cost, and the honest reason the workaround is not good enough. Then generate three user stories in the exact format 'As a [specific role], I want [real capability] so that [human outcome]' — one for the core job, one for a secondary use case, and one for a power-user edge case. For each story, flag if the outcome is technical rather than human, and rewrite it if so. That prompt fills both 2A and 2B in one shot. After it runs, check: does the role in the stories match 2A? Does the 2A user match the Part 1 sentence you wrote in E1? If either check fails, update them before moving to Part 3.

props.segment: Name the Human: User + Problem + 3 Stories
props.command: Here is my A2 problem statement: [paste it]. Here is my A2 user: [paste it]. Generate Part 2A (job title, situation, workaround with tools+steps+time, honest cost) and 2B (3 stories in exact format: 'As a [specific role], I want [capability] so that [human outcome]' — core job, secondary use case, power-user edge). Flag any technical outcomes and rewrite them.
props.runningText: fill in your A2 problem and user before running — the profile only works with real source material…

beats.narration_text: Name the Human: User + Problem + 3 Stories. Liam, in for Bear.
